coredata.dc:title: Sensitive sensing of enzyme-regulated biocatalytic reactions using gold nanoclusters-melanin-like polymer nanosystem
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Xu
Li, Hongxia
Jin, Rui
Zhao, Xu
Liu, Fangmeng
Lu, Geyu
coredata.dc:description: Precise profiling of biocatalytic transformations is of critical importance for diagnosis and treatment. Herein, taking advantage of tyrosinase-triggered polymerization of tyrosine, a fluorescence universal strategy based on gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) for monitoring H2O2-generated biocatalytic reactions was designed. In this approach, melanin-like polymer facilely formed by virtue of oxidation of tyrosine monomers, which employed as efficient quencher for fluorescence of AuNCs, contributing to a photoinduced electron-transfer mechanism. Such fluorescence responds can be obviously inhibited by H2O2, enabling the sensitive sensing of H2O2 in a cost-effective and time-consuming way. Harnessing well-defined polymer system and H2O2-generated biocatalytic processes enabled sensitive detection of cholesterol and acetylcholine (substrates) with detection limit of 100 nmol L−1 and 500 nmol L−1 in low background and convenient manner. Meanwhile, the present bioassay exhibits excellent specificity to metabolite on account of the specificity of enzyme. This versatile and modular platform incorporates highly efficient catalytic reaction of enzyme into AuNCs@melanin-like polymer system, enhancing its potential uptake into clinical diagnostics for accurate profiling the metabolites that participated in H2O2-generation reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Melanin-like polymer
Photoinduced electron-transfer
Hydrogen peroxide
Metabolite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection procedure for cholesterol and acetylcholine
Fluorescence quenching by enzyme-induced oxidative polymerization
Fluorescence sensing of enzyme-regulated biocatalytic reactions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the principle for the sensing of enzyme-regulated biocatalytic reactions.

(A) One-pot synthesis for AuNCs. (B) The TEM images of AuNCs. (C) The excitation and emission spectra of AuNCs. (D) The UV-VIS absorption spectrum of CEW and AuNCs. (E) The FT-IR spectra of CEW and AuNCs. (F) The photostability of the as-prepared AuNCs within 55 min. (G) The effect of NaCl concentration on FL intensity of AuNCs.

(A) The TEM images of AuNCs@melanin-like polymer. (B) The RLS spectra of tyrosine, TYR, AuNCs and the mixture of tyrosine + TYR + AuNCs. (C) The FL emission spectra of AuNCs, AuNCs + TYR, AuNCs + tyrosine and AuNCs + TYR + tyrosine. (D) The UV-VIS spectra of AuNCs, AuNCs + TYR, AuNCs + tyrosine and AuNCs + TYR + tyrosine. (E) Lifetime of AuNCs/TYR in the absence and presence of tyrosine. (F) The FL spectra of AuNCs/TYR system in the presence of different concentration of tyrosine (0, 0.5, 2.5, 5.0, 10, 20, 25, 30, 40 and 50 μmol L−1).

(A) The UV-VIS spectra of AuNCs + TYR + tyrosine system in the presence and absence of H2O2. (B) The FL emission spectra of AuNCs + TYR + tyrosine system in the presence and absence of H2O2. (C) The FL spectra of AuNCs/TYR/tyrosine system in the presence of different concentration of H2O2 (0, 1.25, 2.5, 5.0, 12.5, 25, 50 and 125 μmol L−1). (D) The linear plot of FH0/ FH toward the logarithm concentration of H2O2. FH0 and FH were the FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine system in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively.

(A) The influence of cholesterol on AuNCs/tyrosine/TYR system. (B) The FL emission spectra of AuNCs + TYR + tyrosine system in the presence of cholesterol, CHOse and the mixture of cholesterol + CHOse. (C) The FL spectra of AuNCs/TYR/tyrosine/CHOse system in the presence of different concentration of cholesterol (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10, 20, 40, 80 and 160 μmol L−1). (D) The FL intensity of the AuNCs/TYR/tyrosine/CHOse system with cholesterol or interfering substances (80 μmol L−1).

(A) The schematic illustration of the proposed platform for ACl. (B) The FL spectra of AuNCs/TYR/tyrosine/AChE/ChOx system in the presence of different concentration of ACl (0, 1.25, 2.5, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 150 μmol L−1). (C) The linear plot of FA0/ FA toward the concentration of ACl. (D) The FL intensity of the AuNCs/TYR/tyrosine/AChE/ChOx system with ACl or interfering substances (100 μmol L−1).

head.ce:title: Sensitive sensing of enzyme-regulated biocatalytic reactions using gold nanoclusters-melanin-like polymer nanosystem

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electron Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electron Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Precise profiling of biocatalytic transformations is of critical importance for diagnosis and treatment. Herein, taking advantage of tyrosinase-triggered polymerization of tyrosine, a fluorescence universal strategy based on gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) for monitoring H2O2-generated biocatalytic reactions was designed. In this approach, melanin-like polymer facilely formed by virtue of oxidation of tyrosine monomers, which employed as efficient quencher for fluorescence of AuNCs, contributing to a photoinduced electron-transfer mechanism. Such fluorescence responds can be obviously inhibited by H2O2, enabling the sensitive sensing of H2O2 in a cost-effective and time-consuming way. Harnessing well-defined polymer system and H2O2-generated biocatalytic processes enabled sensitive detection of cholesterol and acetylcholine (substrates) with detection limit of 100 nmol L−1 and 500 nmol L−1 in low background and convenient manner. Meanwhile, the present bioassay exhibits excellent specificity to metabolite on account of the specificity of enzyme. This versatile and modular platform incorporates highly efficient catalytic reaction of enzyme into AuNCs@melanin-like polymer system, enhancing its potential uptake into clinical diagnostics for accurate profiling the metabolites that participated in H2O2-generation reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme, which known as nature's catalyst, is responsible for regulating almost all biochemical processes with precise specificity in living organisms [1,2]. It can catalyze specific substrate to corresponding product, which recognized as key process in the biological metabolism pathway. Imbalance of enzyme may lead to disorder of metabolite, even cause tissue damage and various undesired physiological consequences [3,4]. In particular, metabolite hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), one of the major reactive oxygen species, act as an indispensable oxidative stress indicator in mediating variety of biological processes [5,6]. Therefore, sensitive monitoring of enzyme activity and quantitative profiling of metabolites is of crucial importance in the field of diagnosis and healthcare. Among diverse detection strategies, fluorometric sensors that provide distinct advantages in terms of sensitivity and selectivity, have obtained significant attention [7]. Fluorogenic small molecule probes (such as cyanine fluorochromes [8], peroxy orange [9] and boronate ester [10]) and fluorogenic nanomaterials (quantum dots [11,12], carbon dots [13,14], and graphene quantum dot [15,16]) have been reported for sensing enzyme activity and metabolite in a sensitive manner. Although these strategies possess good performance for sensing, most small molecule probes usually suffered from the limitation of poor water solubility, low selectivity and long reaction time. Furthermore, most of their fluorescence emissions of nanomaterials were located in the range of 400–550 nm, which were obviously affected by biorelated autofluorescence. And most sensing strategies were “turn-off” model with high background interference. Thus, the invention of a sensitive fluorogenic enzyme biosensor with high selectivity and low background, which is water-soluble and fast responsive, remains a considerable challenge.

As a promising fluorescence nanomaterial, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have recently emerged as a versatile candidate in the fabrication of chem/biosensors for the profiling of diverse species, ranging from biomolecules to pollutants [17,18]. Previous knowledge demonstrated that AuNCs exhibit outstanding advantages including superior resistance to photobleaching and good biocompatibility [19]. Especially, the one-step green synthesis [20] with facile-to-use and time-saving makes AuNCs significantly attractive. More importantly, various proteins (bovine serum albumin [21], trypsin [22], and horseradish peroxidase [23]) worked as chelating agents can be employed for synthesizing AuNCs with less environmental impact, hugely boosting its rapid development. Recently, our group used chicken egg white (CEW) as template and stabilizer to guide the formation of the AuNCs without use of any toxic surfactants and organic solvent as well as oxidizing agent [24].

Inspired by the above breakthrough, we proposed a facile and cost-effective fluorometric platform for monitoring metabolite H2O2 by using CEW encapsulated AuNCs (Scheme 1 ). In this paper, the formation of melanin-like polymers with unique optical properties (a broad-band absorbance) can absorb AuNCs, effectively quenching the fluorescence of AuNCs. While upon H2O2 introduction, the polymerization of tyrosine monomers can be inhibited, rapidly causing the subsequent increase of platform FL. Analysis of the FL responds can be performed to monitoring H2O2 or H2O2-generated biocatalytic reactions (e.g. choline oxidase-catalyzed choline oxidation, acetylcholinesterase and choline oxidase-mediated reaction of acetylcholine). Benefiting from the sensitive fluorescence response and low background interference of platform, we developed a versatile bioassay for the sensing of H2O2-generated biocatalytic reactions, thereby revealing the great potential of nanosystem for metabolite in clinical application. Furthermore, this convenient strategy would also be appropriate for synthesis of other fluorescence nanomaterials anchored melanin-like polymer in wide prospect.

Tyrosinase (TYR), cholesterol, cholesterol oxidase (CHOse), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), choline oxidase (ChOx), acetylcholine chloride (ACl), tyrosine and hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) hydrate (HAuCl4·xH2O) of analytical grade were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), glycine, histidine, arginine, phenylalanine, threonine, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and acid phosphatase (ACP) were purchased from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. The product numbers for chemicals were shown in the supporting information. The water with an electrical resistance of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 were used in this study.

A Philips Tecnai F20 electron microscope was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. The FL spectra and UV-VIS absorption were recorded with RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and UV-1700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), separately. All solution pH was tested by a PHS-3C pH meter (Tuopu Co., China).

The preparation of AuNCs using CEW as template followed our previous study [24]. Briefly, 10 mmol L−1 of HAuCl4 solution (5.0 mL) and 50 mg mL-1 of CEW solution (5.0 mL) were mixed for 2.0 min at 37 °C. Then, 1.0 mol L−1 of NaOH solution (0.5 mL) was used to adjust the reaction pH to ∼12, following by further incubation at 37 °C for 12 h. The as-obtained solution was purified with a dialysis bag (MWCO ∼ 1.0 kDa) and dialyzed for 24 h to remove small molecules. After that, the purified solution was freeze-dried, and then dissolved with deionized water to obtain fixed concentration of AuNCs (2.5 mg mL-1). Finally, the as-prepared AuNCs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

AuNCs@melanin-like polymer were prepared through a simple mixing of AuNCs and tyrosine in the presence of tyrosinase. Briefly, 2.5 mg mL−1 of AuNCs and 25 U mL−1 of TYR were mixed with different amount of tyrosine to synthesize AuNCs@melanin-like polymer in 100 mmol L−1 Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.5). The mixture was then incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The structure and optical property were measured to investigate the evolution of the size, morphology, absorption, and FL properties.

Various concentrations of H2O2 (100 μL) were mixed with 25 U mL−1 of TYR (100 μL), 0.8 mmol L−1 of tyrosine (100 μL) and 2.5 mg mL−1 of AuNCs (100 μL) in the presence of 100 mmol L−1 of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.5) for 60 min at 37 °C. Then, the mixtures were diluted to 2.0 mL with ultrapure water and mixed thoroughly before the FL spectra were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection procedure for cholesterol and acetylcholine
ce:section.ce:para: (A) Sensing of cholesterol. Different concentrations of cholesterol (50 μL) was incubated with CHOse (50 μL, 5 U mL−1) in the presence of 10 mmol L−1 of Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 μL, pH 8.5) at 37 °C for 40 min to generate H2O2. Then, the mixture of tyrosine (100 μL, 0.8 mmol L−1), TYR (100 μL, 25 U mL−1), AuNCs (100 μL, 2.5 mg mL−1) and 100 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (7.4, 100 mmol L−1) was added to the above reaction solution and was diluted with deionized water to 2.0 mL. After 60 min, the FL spectrum was recorded for cholesterol detection. (B) Sensing of ACh. 50 μL different concentrations of ACh was incubated with AChE (50 μL, 1 U mL−1) and ChOx (50 μL, 2 U mL−1) in the presence of 10 mmol L−1 of Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 μL, pH 8.0) at 37 °C for 30 min to yield H2O2. Then, the mixture of tyrosine (100 μL, 0.8 mmol L−1), TYR (100 μL, 25 U mL−1), AuNCs (100 μL, 2.5 mg mL−1) and 100 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (7.4, 100 mmol L−1) was added to the above reaction solution and was diluted with deionized water to 2.0 mL. After 60 min, the FL spectrum was recorded using excitation at 500 nm.

CEW contains rich proteins which make it to be excellent template to guide the formation of nanoclusters [25]. The synthesis of AuNCs can be accomplished by incubating CEW and HAuCl4 in alkaline solution at 37 °C for 12 h through a one-pot process without use of any toxic surfactants and organic solvent as well as oxidizing agent (Fig. 1 A). As depict in Fig. 1B, the as-synthesized AuNCs roughly presented a spherical shape with an average diameter of 1–3 nm. The optical properties of AuNCs were also confirmed by FL, UV-VIS and FT-IR spectroscopy. From the FL spectra of AuNCs (Fig. 1C), the typical excitation and emission wavelengths of AuNCs were 500 nm and 607 nm, which possess large Stokes shift of 107 nm, efficiently avoiding the interference between excitation and emission signals. As shown in Fig. 1D, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNCs was diﬀ ;erent from CEW in the wavelength ranging 250–500 nm. While the FT-IR spectra of AuNCs were consistent with CEW, indicating that AuNCs contained the same functional groups of CEW (Fig. 1E). Those results demonstrated that AuNCs were successfully prepared by employing common CEW as precursor. The photostability and chemical stability is critical for fabrication biosensor in practical applications. For photostability, the emission intensity of AuNCs was investigated under 500 nm excitation for 55 min. From Fig. 1F, we clearly observed that the emission intensity of AuNCs is extremely stable, preserving ∼90% of the initial FL intensity after 55 min of continuous intensive excitation. Such excellent photostability was due to the composition and surface structure of AuNCs. Furthermore, the chemical stability of AuNCs was measured under different concentrations of salt. It is obviously revealed in Fig. 1G that the FL intensity of AuNCs kept almost constant in a wide salt concentration range from 0 mmol L−1 to 100 mmol L−1, implying that AuNCs hold good salt stability. Since it had shown good photostability and salt stability, the AuNCs were expected to fabricate biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching by enzyme-induced oxidative polymerization
ce:section.ce:para: The designed nanoplatform consists of AuNCs and melanin-like polymer, where AuNCs were acted as FL indicator and melanin-like polymer were served as quencher. In this system, tyrosinase (TYR) efficiently catalyze the oxidation of tyrosine to produce corresponding quinone, accompanying the intra/intermolecular cross-linking reactions in formation of melanin-like oligomers (Fig. S1) [26–28]. Then the oligomers can spontaneously form melanin-like polymer on the surface of AuNCs, which can be clearly confirmed with TEM image (Fig. 2 A). Furthermore, the functional groups on the surface of melanin-like polymer were characterized with FT-IR spectra. As revealed in Fig. S2, the new peak that appeared at 1060 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C–N, indicating that there was melanin-like polymer in nanocomposite. From resonance light scattering (RLS) spectra, it can be clearly observed that the scattering intensities at 345 nm, 450 nm and 590 nm became stronger in the presence of TYR and tyrosine (Fig. 2B), definitely implying the formation of melanin-like polymer with large diameter. The active product (melanin-like polymer) can function as favorable electron acceptors, contributing to a photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) [29]. The PET effect of melanin-like polymer can be clearly distinguished in FL spectrum. As shown in Fig. 2C, AuNCs exhibited the maximal emission peak at 607 nm, which cannot be affected by TYR or tyrosine, separately (Fig. S3). Whereas, due to the formation of high absorbance of melanin-like polymer in visible region (Fig. 2D), the FL emission of AuNCs can be significantly quenched (Fig. 2C). The trends of FL intensity change were consistent with its color change of solution (Fig. 2C, Inset). For better explanation of the mechanism, the FL lifetime of AuNCs/TYR probe in the presence of diﬀ ;erent concentration of tyrosine were studied (Fig. 2E). The AuNCs/TYR had two components including 0.94 μs (35.53%) and 2.90 μs (64.47%), and the average lifetime was 2.60 μs. After reacting with tyrosine (5, 10 and 20 μmol L−1), the lifetime of AuNCs/TYR were gradually shortened from 2.60 to 2.18 μs. Such remarkable decrease in FL intensity and lifetime corroborated the efficient dynamic quenching process. The corresponding quenching performances of tyrosine (5, 10, 50 and 100 μmol L−1) toward AuNCs/TYR probe were investigated. As revealed in Fig. S4, the fluorescence quenching efficiency can reach 80% after introducing of 100 μmol L−1 tyrosine. The outstanding quenching efficiency can be attributed to the larger surface area of melanin-like polymer and the stronger optical absorption in visible region. To further investigate the PET effect between AuNCs and melanin-like polymer, the FL spectrum of AuNCs was measured by employing diﬀ ;erent concentrations of melanin-like polymer which controlled by tyrosine. As expected, under optimum condition (Fig. S5), the FL intensity at 607 nm was gradually decreased with the addition of tyrosine from 0 to 50 μmol L−1 (Fig. 2F). The calibration equation was FQ0/FQ = 1.032 + 0.045 Log [Tyrosine], μmol L−1, with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.993. FQ0 and FQ were the FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR system in the absence and presence of tyrosine, respectively. When fixed the tyrosine concentration, the increasing of TYR also can induce the quenching of FL intensity (Fig. S6), also confirming the PET process. Furthermore, as depict in Fig. S7, the FL intensity of AuNCs@melanin-like polymer nanosystem could stabilize during a 60-min irradiation process. Therefore, all the above results suggested that the FL intensity of AuNCs can be regulated by the formation of melanin-like polymer.

With inspiration from the inhibition property of H2O2 to polymerization reaction of tyrosine [30], this enzyme-triggered FL quenching can be effectively inhibited by H2O2. The absorbance behaviors of the AuNCs@melanin-like polymer nanosystem in the absence and presence of H2O2 was investigated using UV-VIS spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, the absorbance intensity of AuNCs@melanin-like polymer in visible region was decreased with H2O2, which induced color change of solution from gray to colorless, indicating that H2O2 can effectively prevent tyrosine monomers from polymerizing into melanin-like polymer. The inhibition effect can be also clearly verified by drastically distinct FL of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine solution with or without H2O2. From Fig. 3B, with addition of H2O2, the quenching FL had been alleviated duo to the decrease of melanin-like polymer decorated on the surface of the AuNCs. While different concentration of H2O2 (from 0 to 200 μmol L−1) did not cause notable emission change of AuNCs (Fig. S8), ensuring the feasibility of strategy for sensing H2O2. Thus, the inhibition effect on FL emission quenching of the AuNCs@melanin-like polymer nanosystem depended on the concentration of H2O2, thereby opening a new insight for facile and sensitive detection of H2O2. Under optimum condition (Fig. S9), the FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine/H2O2 solution was observed to enhance linearly (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.991) with H2O2 concentration from 0 to 125 μmol L−1 (Fig. 3C). The calibration equation was FH0/FH = 0.963–0.206 Log [H2O2], μmol L−1 (Fig. 3D). FH0 and FH were the FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine system in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively. Furthermore, we further investigated the FL response of AuNCs@melanin-like polymer nanosystem to 50 μmol L−1 H2O2 in the presence of coexistence substances, including ions, amino acids and protein. As shown in Fig. S10, with the presence of common coexistence substance, AuNCs@melanin-like polymer system was unaffected for the monitoring of H2O2, suggesting the proposed platform carry out well ability of selectivity and resisting interference. In comparison with previous sensors [31–33], the AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine platform possesses high sensitivity and higher anti-interference ability. Therefore, the AuNCs@melanin-like polymer system can be used to quantitative detection of H2O2 in a quenching/inhibition format.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of enzyme-regulated biocatalytic reactions
ce:section.ce:para: Bestowed with good analytical performance, the H2O2-responsive FL system can be expanded as a universal platform to detect metabolites that were specifically catalytic oxidation by their corresponding oxidoreductases to yield H2O2. As the content of H2O2 in the sensing systems was regulated by metabolites, the system offer a quantitative output signal for the corresponding metabolites. As a proof of concept, cholesterol was chosen as a model analyte, considering that it is closely associated with the occurrence of atherosclerosis and hypocholesterolemia [34,35]. In this sensing system, cholesterol oxidase (CHOse) catalyzes the oxidation of cholesterol to produce H2O2, triggering the emission enhance of proposed platform. Note that cholesterol possess little interference to AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine system (Fig. 4 A). Whereas, in the presence of cholesterol and CHOse, H2O2 were produced which can inhibited the formation of polymer, further induced the enhancement of FL intensity, as depict in Fig. 4B. Fig. 4C displayed the FL spectra corresponding to cholesterol in the presence of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine probe and CHOse. With the increase of cholesterol concentration (from 0.2–160 μmol L−1), the generation of H2O2 was enhanced which can inhibit the polymerization of tyrosine, leading to the gradual increase of FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine system. That is, the correlation between FL intensity change and cholesterol concentration was dose-dependent. Under optimum conditions (Fig. S11), there is an excellent linear relationship (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.990) between the FL ratio (FC0/FC) versus the logarithm of cholesterol concentration. The calibration equation was FC0/FC = 0.885–0.1578 Log [cholesterol], μmol L−1 (Fig. 4 C Inset). FC0 and FC were the FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine/ CHOse system in the absence and presence of cholesterol, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 100 nmol L−1, which hold comparable or even better performance comparing with other reported strategies (Table S1). On the other hand, Fig. 4 D showed the FL enhancement response of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine/CHOse system toward cholesterol and other common interferences at the level of 80 μmol L−1, indicating that the proposed platform exhibits a specific recognize to cholesterol on account of the substrate specificity of CHOse. To further investigate the practice applications, the recovery test was conducted by employing the standard addition method. As displayed in Table S2, recoveries of spiked cholesterol in human serum were obtained between 91.4 and 112.5% with the relative standard deviations lower than 5.3%, demonstrating the potential applicability of the proposed strategy for cholesterol detection in real samples.

To further validate the multiuse ability of the AuNCs@melanin-like polymer platform, we explored bi-enzyme cascade catalytic process that involve H2O2-generated oxidase. This was exemplified in Fig. 5 A with the quantitative analysis of acetylcholine (ACl) using a bi-enzyme consisting of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (ChOx). ACl as an important neurotransmitter are involved in the human central and peripheral nervous systems [36]. Due to ACl play an essential role in several diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease [37,38], its relevance in health has been increasingly recognized. In this process, AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of ACl into choline that was subsequently converted by ChOx to produce H2O2 in the presence of oxygen. As the number of H2O2 increased, the FL emission signal was partially restored. To obtain good performance, optimizations of the usage concentrations of AChE and ChOx were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S12, the maximum enhancement of FL intensity was observed with the presence of 2 U mL−1 ChOx and 1 U mL−1 AChE. Then, the sensing performances for ACl using AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine/AChE/ChOx system were carried out on the basis of the optimized conditions. Different concentrations of ACl were added to the established platform and the FL emission were collected in Fig. 5B. Experiments reveal that the FL enhanced with an increase in the ACl concentration. As expected, the FL intensity of ratios (FA0 /FA) was linear against logarithm of ACl, which possessed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.994) in the range of 1.25–150 μmol L-1 (Fig. 5C). FA0 and FA were the FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine/AChE/ChOx system in the absence and presence of ACl, respectively. This dual enzyme amplification step increases the detection sensitivity and lowers the LOD (500 nmol L-1 for ACl at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which was comparable to or even better than those of previous literature [39–43]. Looking toward the challenges of practical application, we examined both the specificity and anti-interference ability. A control experiment by replacing ACl with other common interference were investigated in Fig. 5D. Only ACl caused a remarkable increase in FL intensity of AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine/AChE/ChOx system, implying that the proposed biosensor revealed satisfactory specificity of for ACl detection.

As displayed in Fig. 5D, even in the presence of these coexistence substance, the sensing system was unaffected for the determination of ACl, indicating that the AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine/AChE/ChOx platform carry out well ability of resisting interference. Taken together, the as-proposed AuNCs/TYR/Tyrosine system, containing low background and high PET efficiency, is highly desired as a universal platform for accurate monitoring those H2O2-producing enzyme catalytic reactions effectively and detecting the corresponding enzyme substrates.

In summary, taking advantage of TYR-triggered polymerization of tyrosine, well-defined AuNCs@melanin-like polymer platform was easily constructed for the accurate detection of H2O2 in highly sensitive manner. In comparison with previous assays, this sensor possessed great selectivity based on the specificity of enzyme and recognition ability of melanin-like polymer. Such outstanding property enables the probing of metabolite by virtue of biocatalytic oxidase-stimulated H2O2-generating with low background interference. Based on H2O2-responsive FL of the AuNCs@melanin-like polymer nanosystem, a linear range of 0.2–160 μmol L−1 to cholesterol was obtained with the LOD of 100 nmol L−1. Moreover, the successful monitoring of ACl was further achieved to probe bi-enzyme biocatalytic cascades of AChE and ChOx. Structural combination of melanin-like polymer with AuNCs provides newly formed nanocomposites with high synergistic properties and excellent stability, which were expected to fabricate microfluidic devices for preclinical diagnosis using paper-based chip, further extending their practical application. Overall, the conceptual integration of enzymatic reactions with AuNCs@melanin-like polymer nanosystem not only opening up a new avenue for sensing of H2O2-related enzymatic reactions, but also provides a promising design for clinical application.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX201700096), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (No. 2017M621199), Program for JLU Science and Technology Innovative Research Team and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Small

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xu Yan received his M.S. degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University. He joined the group of Prof. Xingguang Su at Jilin University and received his Ph.D. degree in June 2017. Since then, he did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu and Prof. Junqiu Liu. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Hongxia Li received her M.S. degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University. and received her Ph.D. degree in 2016 at Jilin University. Since then, she did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu. Currently, her research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Rui Jin received her M.S. degree in 2016 from College of Chemistry, Jilin University, China. Currently, she is studying for her Ph.D. degree in College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University. Her research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Xu Zhao received the B.Eng.degree in department of electronic sciences and technology,Jilin University in 2016.Now,he is studying for his MS degree in College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Fangmeng Liu received his PhD degree in 2017 from College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China. Now he is a lecturer of Jilin University, China. His current research interests include the application of functional materials and development of solid state electrolyte gas sensor and flexible device.

Geyu Lu received the B. Sci. degree in electronic sciences in 1985 and the M. Sci. degree in 1988 from Jilin University in China and the Dr. Eng. degree in 1998 from Kyushu University in Japan. Now he is a professor of Jilin University, China. His current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and the application of the function materials.
